teaching_arc.entry_point: Students know antiderivatives (Topic 22) and the definite integral as a Riemann limit (Topics 23–24) but still fear computing integrals by hand.
teaching_arc.progression: Riemann hook → speedometer/odometer → FTC Part 1 theorem → FTC Part 2 theorem → Part 2 warm-up → Part 1 standard → chain-rule misconception → chain-rule worked example → edge cases & apps → visual lab → pause pair → optional proof → recap with substitution tease.
teaching_arc.destination: Students evaluate definite integrals via F(b)-F(a), differentiate integrals with variable limits (chain rule when needed), and know which FTC part to use.
teaching_arc.tone: Clear, warm, visual, active, bridge between sums and antiderivatives.
methods: Use riemann_integral to show convergence then FTC shortcut.
theorem_box for both FTC parts before examples.
math_solution_steps for all worked evaluations and derivatives.

audience_level: first-year university / advanced high school, completed Topics 22–24
topic_goal: Teach FTC Part 1 and Part 2 so students connect differentiation and integration and evaluate definite integrals efficiently.
topic_takeaways: FTC Part 2: ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx = F(b)−F(a), no +C needed.
FTC Part 1: d/dx ∫ₐˣ f(t)dt = f(x); chain rule when upper limit is g(x).
Integration and differentiation are inverse processes (rate ↔ accumulation).
Swap limits and negate when the variable is in the lower limit.
Topic 26 (substitution) extends Part 2 to harder antiderivatives.
style_notes: Welcome back, do NOT re-introduce Arian from scratch.
Callback Topic 22 antiderivatives and Topic 24 definite integral / Riemann sums.
Roadmap currentId path_topic_25; Topic 26 substitution named only in summary.
Do not teach substitution before Part 2 is solid.
Pause slides s13–s14 are a real think beat, not decorative.
When riemann widget present, narrate n increasing then pivot to FTC exact value.
Avatar low on dense math_solution_steps slides.
transition_style: Hook with tedious sums → promise shortcut → formal parts → examples → lab → pause → recap.

slide_ref.source_title: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus – Connecting Integration and Differentiation

slides.narration_seed: Welcome back, Topic 25 bridges antiderivatives and definite integrals. FTC turns infinite sums into endpoint subtraction.
must_cover: Topic 25 in Session 5 after definite integral.
Callbacks: Topic 22 antiderivatives, Topic 24 Riemann limit definition.
Today: FTC Part 1 and Part 2, not substitution yet.
must_say: We defined the definite integral as a limit of sums, today we get the shortcut.
slides.transition_from_previous: Topic 24 ended with net area and the limit definition; this topic delivers the evaluation shortcut.
likely_student_confusion: Thinking FTC replaces understanding of area, tie back to Riemann intuition.
slides.explanation_style: Brief welcome-back, name the bridge between Topics 22 and 24.
slides.story_hint: Fast-forward button after counting every Riemann rectangle.
slides.delivery_goal: Students know FTC is the payoff for the integration arc so far.

slides.narration_seed: Roadmap current Topic 25. Five skills: Part 2 evaluation, inverse interpretation, Part 1 with chain rule, integral-defined functions, applications.
must_cover: Roadmap before bullets, path_topic_25 current, path_topic_26 next.
Five objectives map to Part 2, Part 1, trap, applications.
slides.transition_from_previous: From title hook into structured promise.
slides.delivery_goal: Students see where FTC sits in Calc 1.

slide_ref.source_title: From Riemann Sums to a Shortcut

slides.narration_seed: Topic 24 limit of sums is correct but tedious. Widget: increase n, sum converges, but FTC will skip the limit. Tease antiderivative at two points.
must_cover: Computing the limit directly is painful.
FTC shortcut tease, two endpoint evaluations.
slides.transition_from_previous: Objectives promised efficiency, show why we need it.
slides.explanation_style: Narrate rectangle convergence, then pivot to shortcut promise.
slides.story_hint: Counting every step vs reading the odometer.
slides.delivery_goal: Students feel the pain FTC removes.

slide_ref.source_title: A Simple Analogy: Speedometer and Odometer

slides.narration_seed: Speedometer = derivative of position; odometer = antiderivative. ∫ₐᵇ v(t)dt = s(b)-s(a). Inverse relationship before Part 1/2 statements.
must_cover: Table: v(t) rate vs s(t) accumulation.
Integration and differentiation undo each other.
slides.transition_from_previous: From abstract area to motion language students recognize.
likely_student_confusion: Confusing displacement with total distance, net change framing.
slides.explanation_style: Car metaphor, then formula on screen.
slides.delivery_goal: Students connect rate and accumulation inverses.

slide_ref.source_title: FTC Part 1 – Derivative of an Integral

slides.narration_seed: theorem_box Part 1: d/dx ∫ₐˣ f(t)dt = f(x). Preview g(x) upper limit → chain rule. Widget: tangent slope on accumulation equals f(x).
must_cover: General form with g(x) upper limit.
Differentiating an integral returns the integrand.
likely_student_confusion: Applying Part 1 when they need Part 2 for numbers.
slides.explanation_style: State theorem, then widget confirms slope = f(x).
slides.story_hint: Accumulation function's slope at x is f(x).
slides.delivery_goal: Students state Part 1 and know when to differentiate an integral.

slides.narration_seed: theorem_box Part 2: ∫ₐᵇ f = F(b)-F(a). +C cancels. Callback Topic 22 antiderivatives. Widget: shaded area = endpoint difference.
slides.transition_from_previous: Part 1 differentiates integrals; Part 2 evaluates them.
likely_student_confusion: Including +C in definite integral bracket notation.
slides.story_hint: Two endpoint evaluations replace infinite rectangles.
slides.delivery_goal: Students can quote Part 2 and explain why +C drops out.

slides.narration_seed: math_solution_steps: ∫₀¹(3x²+2x)dx. step_1 antiderivative x³+x²; step_2 endpoints; step_3 answer 2. Student try ∫₀^{π/4} sec²x.
likely_student_confusion: Use the prompt to surface: Try ∫₀^{π/4} sec²x dx = 1.
slides.explanation_style: Reveal steps in order; bracket notation on right.
slides.story_hint: First FTC Part 2 win, no Riemann sum.
slides.delivery_goal: Students execute F(b)-F(a) on a polynomial.

slides.narration_seed: g(x)=∫₀ˣ 1/(1+t²)dt. step_1 identify f; step_2 g'(x)=f(x); step_3 g'(x)=1/(1+x²), g'(2)=1/5. No antiderivative needed.
must_cover: math_solution_steps Part 1 on plain upper limit x.
Contrast: Part 2 needs F; Part 1 does not.
slides.transition_from_previous: Part 2 warm-up done, mirror with Part 1.
likely_student_confusion: Try g(x)=∫₀ˣ √(1+t³) dt → g'(2)=√5/32 approx, surface Part 1 pattern.
slides.explanation_style: Short flow steps; emphasize no arctan required.
slides.delivery_goal: Students apply Part 1 when upper limit is x.

slide_ref.source_title: Misconception: Forgetting the Chain Rule in FTC Part 1

slides.narration_seed: misconception_compare: wrong cos(x²) vs correct cos(x²)·2x. Callback Topic 13 chain rule. General rule f(g(x))·g'(x).
slides.transition_from_previous: Plain x limit was easy, variable limit needs chain rule.
likely_student_confusion: Dropping g'(x) is the #1 Part 1 error.
slides.explanation_style: Wrong vs right side-by-side before worked example.
slides.story_hint: Wrong tangent line does not match G(x).

slide_ref.source_title: Tricky Example: FTC Part 1 with Chain Rule

slides.narration_seed: h(x)=∫₀^{x³} sin(t²)dt. u=x³; h'(x)=sin(u²)·3x²=3x²sin(x⁶). step_1 u; step_2 chain; step_3 substitute.
slides.transition_from_previous: Misconception named, now full worked chain case.
slides.transition_to_next: Edge case variable lower limit s09.
slides.explanation_style: Three-step flow; formula template on right.
slides.story_hint: Same pattern as s07 with harder g(x).

slides.narration_seed: p(x)=∫_x^5 e^{t²}dt. Swap to -∫_5^x; p'(x)=-e^{x²}. Shrinking interval → negative derivative makes sense.
slides.transition_from_previous: Upper limit tricks done, handle lower limit.

slides.narration_seed: F'=3x², F(1)=5. Method 1: x³+C, C=4. Method 2: F(1)+∫₁ˣ 3t² dt. Both give x³+4.
slides.transition_from_previous: Derivative of integrals → rebuild function from derivative.
likely_student_confusion: Try F'=cos x, F(π)=0 → F(x)=sin x.
slides.story_hint: FTC connects to differential equations preview.
slides.delivery_goal: Students see Part 2 as accumulation from a known value.

slides.narration_seed: Area between y=x and y=x² on [0,1]. ∫₀¹(x-x²)dx = [x²/2-x³/3]₀¹ = 1/6. Top minus bottom.
slides.story_hint: Part 2 pays off on area problems.
slides.delivery_goal: Students set up and evaluate area between curves.

slides.narration_seed: visual_lab riemann_integral. Increase n, sum approaches exact area = F(b)-F(a). Callback Topics 23–24. Student: a=0.5, b=2, n=30.
must_cover: Riemann sum converges; FTC is exact.
likely_student_confusion: Set a=0.5, b=2, n=30, how close is sum to FTC value?
slides.explanation_style: Let widget run; narrate error shrinking.
slides.delivery_goal: Students connect sum limit to F(b)-F(a).

slides.narration_seed: Pause: g(x)=∫₀^{x²} 1/(1+t²)dt, find g'(x). Do not reveal, hint u=x². Real YouTube pause beat.
must_say: Pause the video and try it yourself.
slides.transition_from_previous: Lab reinforced Part 2, test Part 1 chain skill.
slides.explanation_style: Pose problem, pause, no full answer.
slides.story_hint: Mirror s08 pattern with rational integrand.

slides.narration_seed: math_solution_steps: u=x²; g'(x)=1/(1+u²)·2x=2x/(1+x⁴). Did you get it? Pair with s13.
slides.explanation_style: Reveal steps in order after student attempt.

slide_ref.source_title: [Challenge – Optional] Proof Sketch for FTC Part 1

slides.narration_seed: Optional proof_sketch: g'(x)=lim (1/h)∫_x^{x+h} f ≈ f(x)·h. Widget: shrink interval. Skip if time-limited.
slides.delivery_goal: Interested students see why Part 1 is true.

slides.narration_seed: Recap Part 1 & 2, chain trap, inverse link. Tease Topic 26 Integration by Substitution. Final check ∫₀² x² dx = 8/3.
likely_student_confusion: Which part to differentiate an integral-defined function? Answer: Part 1.
slides.explanation_style: Five bullets + final mental FTC Part 2 check.
slides.story_hint: FTC closes the bridge; substitution handles harder integrands.
slides.delivery_goal: Students leave with Part 1/2 workflow and next-topic preview.
